coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen dots as reductant and stabilizer for the synthesis of AgNPs/N-dots nanocomposites for efficient surface-enhanced Raman scattering detection
coredata.dc:creator: Lin, Zhenhua
Yang, Jiani
Tang, Zhijiao
Li, Gongke
Hu, Yuling
coredata.dc:description: A simple method was developed for the preparation of silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) by using amino-functionalized nitrogen dots (N-dots) as reductant and stabilizer under mild conditions. N-dots were hydrothermal synthesized using 2-azidoimidazole as the precursor in aqueous ammonia. The as prepared N-dots were rich in nitrogen and oxygen-containing functional groups and exhibit strong reducing capacity. Therefore, AgNPs were rapidly synthesized under mild conditions based on N-dots serving as the reducing agent, the reaction time and temperature were 5min and 40°C, respectively. Moreover, owing to the abundant functional groups on the surface of N-dots, the obtained AgNPs/N-dots nanocomposites with the average diameters of 12.9nm were well dispersed and stable at 4°C for at least two months. Furthermore, the synthesized AgNPs/N-dots showed high surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) activity and provided a sensitive SERS sensing ability for malachite green (MG) and crystal violet (CV) in aquaculture water, with the low detection limit (S/N = 3) of 2.7nmol/L and 2.0nmol/L, respectively, corresponding with the wide linear range from 10.0 to 1000.0nmol/L for MG and 5.0–100.0nmol/L for CV. This method offers an easy and low-cost way to prepare AgNPs/N-dots substrates and makes SERS detection more practicable.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen dots
Silver nanoparticles
Mild conditions
Surface-enhanced Raman scattering
Aquaculture water

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: SERS detection of MG and CV in aquaculture water

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21475153 , 21575167 and 21675178 ), the Guangdong Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 2015A030311020 and 2016A030313358 ), and the Special Funds for Public Welfare Research and Capacity Building in Guangdong Province of China (No. 2015A030401036 ), and the Guangzhou Science and Technology Program of China (No. 201604020165 ), respectively. Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of preparation of AgNPs/N-dots nanocomposites.

The Raman intensity of AgNPs/N-dots obtained from (A) different volume ratio of N-dots and AgNO3 (from a to h are 0:5, 4:5, 8:5, 12:5, 14:5, 16:5, 18:5, 20:5, respectively), (B) different time (a) and temperature (b). All the error bars were calculated based on the standard deviation of three measurements.
TEM images of N-dots (A) and the AgNPs/N-dots nanocomposites (B).

(A) UV–vis spectrum and the corresponding images (inset) of AgNO3 (a), N-dots (b) and AgNPs/N-dots (c), respectively; (B) Fluorescence spectra of N-dots (a) and AgNPs/N-dots (b). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).
XPS spectra of N-dots (A) and AgNPs/N-dots nanocomposites (B); C 1s XPS spectra of N-dots (C) and AgNPs/N-dots nanocomposites (D); N 1s XPS spectra of N-dots (E) and AgNPs/N-dots nanocomposites (F).

(A) SERS spectra of CV solution which was selected from 42 points randomly within a whole substrate area; (B) UV–vis spectra of AgNPs/N-dots nanocomposites with the preservation of the different save time.

SERS spectra obtained from the assays of different concentration of MG (A) and CV (B). Inset describe the linear relationships between peak intensities at ∼ 1617 (or 1619cm−1) and the concentration of MG (or CV). The error bars reflect the standard deviations from three repetitive experiments.
Comparison of the synthesis method of AgNPs/N-dots with other nanomaterials.
Assignments of vibrational bands of malachite green and crystal violet [32,33].
Analytical results for the determination of samples.

row.entry: The aquaculture water of food fish
The aquaculture water of ornamental fish
The aquaculture water of food fish
The aquaculture water of ornamental fish

head.ce:title: Nitrogen dots as reductant and stabilizer for the synthesis of AgNPs/N-dots nanocomposites for efficient surface-enhanced Raman scattering detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry, Sun Yat-sen University, Guangzhou 510275, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry, Sun Yat-sen University

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contribute equally to the work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A simple method was developed for the preparation of silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) by using amino-functionalized nitrogen dots (N-dots) as reductant and stabilizer under mild conditions. N-dots were hydrothermal synthesized using 2-azidoimidazole as the precursor in aqueous ammonia. The as prepared N-dots were rich in nitrogen and oxygen-containing functional groups and exhibit strong reducing capacity. Therefore, AgNPs were rapidly synthesized under mild conditions based on N-dots serving as the reducing agent, the reaction time and temperature were 5min and 40°C, respectively. Moreover, owing to the abundant functional groups on the surface of N-dots, the obtained AgNPs/N-dots nanocomposites with the average diameters of 12.9nm were well dispersed and stable at 4°C for at least two months. Furthermore, the synthesized AgNPs/N-dots showed high surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) activity and provided a sensitive SERS sensing ability for malachite green (MG) and crystal violet (CV) in aquaculture water, with the low detection limit (S/N = 3) of 2.7nmol/L and 2.0nmol/L, respectively, corresponding with the wide linear range from 10.0 to 1000.0nmol/L for MG and 5.0–100.0nmol/L for CV. This method offers an easy and low-cost way to prepare AgNPs/N-dots substrates and makes SERS detection more practicable.

ce:section.ce:para: Surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) has become a nondestructive, ultrasensitive, and trace species detection technique for structural characterizations and sensing, and therefore SERS has gained significant attention in recent years [1–4]. Nano-structured metal surfaces or metal nano-particles (NPs) have been used as SERS-active substrates, which SERS activity influences largely the extent of SERS applications. Among the investigated noble metals, Ag nanoparticles (AgNPs) are one of the most outstanding star materials due to their high signal enhancement, strong plasmonic ability and easy preparation [5]. However, it should be noted that the easy oxidation of AgNPs surface is an intrinsic disadvantage, which will seriously affect its application in real systems [6]. Therefore, it is a challenge to improve the stability of AgNPs for their analytical applications.

Recently, AgNPs have been fabricated on the surface of carbon nanomaterials such as carbon dots [7], carbon nanotubes [8,9] and graphene quantum dots [10,11] for SERS substrates. By using the gapped metal nano-islands, the carbon nanomaterials were helpful for creating electromagnetic “hot spots”, which is the origin of the main SERS enhancement [7]. On the other hand, carbon quantum dots (C-dots) have emerged as an advanced support material for the synthesis of AgNPs in the last few years [12,13]. Owing to the rich oxygen containing functional groups such as hydroxyl, carbonyl, carboxyl, and epoxy groups on their surfaces, C-dots can serve as an excellent electron acceptor and electron donor, indicating the potential as an oxidizing or reducing agent [14]. Liu et al. synthesized AgNPs by using C-dots as a reducing agent in boiled water (100°C) with NH3·H2O [13]. Li's group successfully fabricated AgNPs/C-dots composite by using C-dots as reducing and stabilizing agent, the C-dots synthesized with polyethyleneimine (PEI) showed an extraordinary reducibility [15]. Chi et al. used C-dots with abundant oxygen-containing functional groups as the coating agent for the preparation of AgNPs [7]. The synthesized AgNPs@C-dots showed high SERS activity and provided a sensitive SERS sensing ability for 4-aminothiophenol. However, these reported methods have some disadvantages regarding the preparation of the reduced C-dots and AgNPs/C-dots. Most of them require a high temperature, as well as complicated and time-consuming procedures. In addition, the poor biocompatibility and less active site of carbon nanomaterials are still the limitations.

More recently, nitrogen-rich quantum dots (N-dots), as a new member of the quantum-dots family, were synthesized in methanol under mild conditions, which demonstrated distinct and unique optical properties. Our previous work presented a facile and quick strategy of synthesizing fluorescent amino nitrogen quantum dots (aN-dots) using 2-azidoimidazole and aqueous ammonia [16]. The as-prepared aN-dots have abundant amino groups, which exhibit good biocompatibility and high fluorescence quantum yield. More importantly, N atom possesses stronger electron-donating ability compared with O atom. Therefore, N-dots with abundant nitrogen and oxygen-containing functional groups show stronger reducing capacity than carbon quantum dots, indicating the potential as reducing agent for the preparation of AgNPs under mild conditions.

Herein, a novel and facile method is proposed for the synthesis of AgNPs/N-dots. Considering that they are excellent electron donors [17,18], N-dots have promising potential to be a reducing agent [19]. N-dots with excellent biocompatibility were hydrothermal synthesized using 2-azidoimidazole as the precursor based on our previous work. The as prepared N-dots were rich in nitrogen and oxygen-containing functional groups on their surface, which could served as electron donors for the reduction of silver ions to element silver within a very short time and under a mild reaction temperature. Meanwhile N-dots protect and stabilize the AgNPs from aggregation in aqueous medium; that is, the AgNPs are stable at least for two months in aqueous medium. Therefore, the amino-rich N-dots can be used as an efficient reductant and stabilizing agent for fast synthesis of AgNPs. Moreover, the as-prepared AgNPs/N-dots nanocomposites exhibited a strong SERS signal with excellent reproducibility. The substrate is used for the highly sensitive detection of malachite green (MG) and crystal violet (CV), which are prohibited drugs in aquatic products for they may pose health risks and adverse environmental impact [20].

Sodium nitrite (NaNO2, 99.8%) and sodium azide (NaN3, 98.5%) were obtained from J&K (Beijing, China). 2-aminoimidazole sulfuric acid (C6H10N6·H2SO4, 96.5%) is the product of Shanghai Shaoyuan Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Aqueous ammonia (NH3·H2O, 25%), hydrochloric acid (HCl, AR), sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO3, AR), ethyl acetate (C4H8O2, AR), sodium sulphate (Na2SO4, AR), silver nitrate (AgNO3, 99.8%), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, AR) were purchased from Guangzhou Chemical Reagent Factory (Guangzhou, China). All other chemicals were of analytical grade and were used as received without further purification.

High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images of N-dots and the AgNPs/N-dots nanocomposites were obtained on a model FEI Tecnai G2 field emission transmission electron microscope (FEI, Netherlands). UV–vis absorption spectra were carried out using a model Cary 300 Conc 134 UV–visible spectrophotometer (Varian, America). Raman spectra were performed on a battery-powered Raman spectrometer (model Inspector Raman, diode laser, excitation wavelength λex = 785nm) in the range of 200–2000cm−1 (DeltaNu, USA). Fluorescence (FL) spectra were collected by a RF-5301PC fluorescence spectrometer (SHIMADZU, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were measured using an ESCAlab 250 XPS system (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA).

In a typical experiment, 1.65g 2-aminoimidazole sulfuric acid salt was dissolved in 10mL HCl (5.0mol/L) and stirred at 0℃ under ice-water bath, followed by the addition of 15mL NaNO2 (0.84mol/L). Then, 5mL of NaN3 solution (2.5mol/L) was also added to the above solution, followed by 20h incubation under stirring to produce the 2-azidoimidazole. After adjusting the pH value of solution to 7.0–7.5 with NaHCO3, the reaction mixture was extracted by ethyl acetate (3 × 25mL). The combined organic layers were dried over anhydrous Na2SO4. After filtration and evaporation, 0.90g of yellow solid residue was obtained, 90.3% yield. The 2-azidoimidazole was characterized by 1H NMR, mass spectrum and FTIR spectra (Supporting information, Figs. S1–S3).

The synthesis of N-dots was performed according to our previous report [16]. Briefly, 0.36g of 2-aminoimidazole was placed in an ammonia solution (75mL, 25%) and heated at 70°C for 24h. After the reaction was complete, ammonia was removed with the helping of the rotary evaporation apparatus. Next, the obtained residue was dispersed in 30mL of deionized water and washed 4–5 times with ethyl acetate solution, followed by centrifugation and filtration to remove larger particles. Finally, the supernatant was diluted with deionized water to 100mL. The resulting N-dots were stored at 4°C until used.

The AgNPs/N-dots were synthesized by a chemical reduction of N-dots/Ag+ complexes. The as-prepared N-dots was diluted to 0.82mg/mL with pure water before used. Briefly, 0.64mL of the diluted N-dots aqueous solution, 2mL of NaOH (70.0mmol/L) and 200μL of AgNO3 (14.0mmol/L) were incubated for 5min at 40°C in a water bath. Subsequently, yellowish-brown AgNPs/N-dots nanocomposites were obtained. The resulting solution was filtered through 0.22µm Millipore membrane, and the AgNPs/N-dots nanocomposites with the size of 12.9nm were stored in a refrigerator at 4°C for further use.

In a typical assay, 25μL AgNPs/N-dots were drop-cast onto the silicon substrate. After evaporation of water at 60°C, then soaking the substrate in 1mL analyte solution and keep 5min to make the analyte fully absorbed on the substrate. The Raman spectra were recorded immediately after the sample preparation. The typical exposure time for each measurement was 1s, the aquaculture water samples were collected from Guangzhou. These samples were directly used for SERS measurement without any sample preparation except for filtering through 0.22µm Millipore membrane.

As schematically shown in Fig. 1, N-dots with abundant amino groups and oxygen-containing functional groups that acted as an electron donor for ensuring the reduction of metal ions to atoms within a very short time and under a mild reaction temperature. To be specific, Ag+ was firstly attracted to the surface of N-dots via electrostatic interactions, and then Ag+ was reduced by N-dots via electron transfer. Ag atoms functioned as the nucleation centers, followed by the growth of metallic nanocrystals, leading to formation of AgNPs. Moreover, N-dots also played the role of a stabilizer during this process, preventing AgNPs from aggregation [21,22].

For best probe performance, we first investigated the effects of synthesis conditions including the volume ratio between N-dots and AgNO3 solutions, reaction temperature and incubation time on AgNPs growth. Due to the good Raman properties and large scattering cross-section [23–25], Rhodamine 6G (R6G) was used as a model molecule to evaluate the SERS performances of the obtained AgNPs/N-dots nanocomposites by modifying them on silicon wafers. Apparently, the N-dots and AgNO3 concentrations are critical to the particle size and quantity of AgNPs. As manifested in Fig. 2A, the Raman intensity increases with the volume ratio of N-dots and AgNO3 from 0:5 to 16:5, which should be related to the fact that the concentration of generated AgNPs is increases as reducing agent increases, and the high concentration of AgNPs was useful for creating electromagnetic “hot spots”. However, when the volume ratio between N-dots and AgNO3 solutions is too high, the Raman intensity will decrease slightly. The reason that SERS signal decreases as the mass ratio of N-dots to AgNO3 increases (i.e. increasing N-dot density on AgNP surfaces) might be that under the uniform morphology conditions, the excessive N-dots at AgNPs prevented the R6G molecules from adsorbing on the surface of AgNPs/N-dots [7]. On the basis of these observations, the most suitable volume ratio of N-dots to AgNO3 solutions was 16:5. The effect of the reaction temperature and incubation time on the growth of AgNPs were further investigated. The Fig. 2B displays that the Raman intensity increase with increasing incubation time in the range from 0 to 5min and do not undergo an obvious change after 5min, suggesting that N-dots have the ability to reduce AgNO3 into Ag and the reaction could finish within 5min, which is evidently shorter than other methods (see Table 1). Therefore, the incubation time was selected as 5min for preparing AgNPs. It is also clear seen from the Fig. 2B, the reaction temperature being too high or too low is not suitable for improving the Raman intensity. Accordingly, 40℃ is adopted as the optimal reaction temperature.

It can be seen from Table 1, the synthesis conditions for preparing AgNPs including incubation time and reaction temperature were superior to others. Such mild conditions of preparation AgNPs is contributed to the using N-dots with abundant nitrogen and oxygen-containing groups as reductant.

In order to observe the microstructure of the N-dots to identify the formation of the AgNPs/N-dots nanocomposites, transmission electron microscope (TEM) was performed. As illustrated in Fig. 3, it is obviously seen that the N-dots were monodisperse and these particles showed the average size is 2.8nm. After reacting completely with AgNO3 in 5min, the average diameters of the as-prepared AgNPs/N-dots is 12.9nm, which exhibiting the larger size than N-dots. The reason might be that the N-dots have been attached to the surface of AgNPs, thereby the formation of the AgNPs/N-dots nanocomposites and the increasing of average diameters.

In addition, the remarkable optical properties of the as-synthesized N-dots and AgNPs/N-dots nanocomposites were characterized by the UV–vis spectra and the fluorescence (FL) spectra. As shown in Fig. 4A, the as-synthesized N-dots and AgNPs/N-dots nanocomposites have characteristic absorption peaks at 320nm and 440nm，respectively. What's more, the FL spectrum of N-dots shows an emission peak at 385nm when excited at 320nm (Fig. 4B), indicating the nanodots are fluorescent material. It is also seen from Fig. 4B that the as prepared N-dots showed a high fluorescent property (curve a). Compared with the original N-dots, the AgNPs/N-dots apparently exhibited a lower fluorescence intensity and a red-shift of 27nm (curve b) as well as the solution color changing from light yellow to brown (the images in Fig. 4A). This might be attributed to the change of the surface states of the N-dots which was induced by the in situ reduction of Ag+ to form AgNPs/N-dots nanocomposites. The obtained AgNPs/N-dots nanocomposites exhibited an apparently lower FL intensity, indicating the low background interference for SERS detection by using AgNPs/N-dots as substrate.

XPS was performed to investigate the surface configuration of N-dots and AgNPs/N-dots nanocomposites. As can be seen from Fig. 5A and Fig. 5B, the survey XPS spectrum clearly indicates the existence of C, N, and O in N-dots, and C, N, O, Ag in AgNPs/N-dots nanocomposites. As also shown in Fig. 5B, the peaks at 373.8eV and 367.8eV are belonged to the binding energies of Ag 3d3/2 and Ag 3d5/2, which agrees well with the theoretical values for metallic silver. These results indicated that the AgNPs/N-dots were successfully formed, which is in agreement with the UV–vis and TEM results. Fig. 5C and Fig. 5D show that there are four main types of C 1s peaks at 284.6, 285.6, 287.9, and 288.5eV in the XPS spectrum of N-dots and AgNPs/N-dots，which were assigned to C-C/C = C, C-N/C-O, C = O and C(O)-O, respectively. One can find that the intensity of C-O peaks in the XPS spectrum of the AgNPs/N-dots nanocomposites, are lower obviously, which indicates that the C-O groups on the surface of N-dots were oxidized during the formation of the AgNPs/N-dots nanocomposites. In addition, an increase in the intensity of the C(O)–O bond was observed, which suggested that some oxygen-containing groups that on the N-dots were oxidized and changed to C(O)-O groups. Moreover, it can clearly find from Fig. 5E that the high resolution N 1s spectrum of N-dots can be divided into three component peaks with binding energies at 399.2, 399.9, and 400.7eV, which were attributed to C-N, C-N-C, and N-H, respectively. In contrast, from the XPS spectrum of N 1s of AgNPs/N-dots in Fig. 5F, we can see that a disappearance of the N 1s peaks at 400.7eV and a decrease in the intensity of the N 1s peaks at 399.9eV, which is consistent with the results of Zhao et al. [31], they infer that the N–H groups may provide excellent stability during the formation of AgNPs due to the electronic charge binding between Ag and N atoms.

The uniformity and stability are important parameters for an applicable SERS substrate. Therefore, we randomly choose 42 separated points on the whole substrate area of 0.42mm2 to investigate their uniformity by using CV as the probe molecule under identical experimental conditions. As shown in Fig. 6A, there are no obvious changes in the characteristic peak position except for very minimal variations in the intensity at various locations for the 42 random points. The relative standard deviations (RSDs) in SERS intensity at three prominent peaks of 916, 1177, and 1620cm−1 are 7.1%, 5.6%, 10.3%, respectively, which demonstrate excellent uniformity of the prepared AgNPs/N-dots substrate. In order to further examine the uniformity of the substrate, we also checked the reproducibility of the SERS measurement by recording spectra from 10 repetitive experiments on the same batch and taking SERS spectra with 5 different batches of the AgNPs/N-dots samples (Fig. S4). The RSDs of the peak at 1620cm−1 are 4.7% and 5.3% respectively, which indicated the AgNPs/N-dots nanocomposites as a SERS substrate displayed high uniformity and good reproducibility.

Furthermore, to assess the stability of AgNPs/N-dots substrate, the substrate was stored at 4°C and ultraviolet spectrum measurements were continuously conducted under the same experimental conditions. It can be seen from Fig. 6B that the absorbance intensity at 440nm in UV–vis spectrums did not change significantly for up to 64 days at 4°C, indicating the long time stability of AgNPs/N-dots substrate. The effects of acidity and ionic strength on the stability of substrate were further investigated in Fig. S5 in the Supporting information. One can find that the optical properties of AgNPs/N-dots was stable while the pH varied from 2 to 12. Moreover, it was also experimentally found that there was no evident changes in absorption curves even in the presence of high-concentration salt solution. The excellent stability of AgNPs/N-dots substrate is believed to be mainly due to the peripheral charges of N-dots surface help to stabilize AgNPs in aqueous solutions and prevent their aggregation [14]. Such superior stability allows potential applications for practical use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: SERS detection of MG and CV in aquaculture water
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the potential of AgNPs/N-dots for quantitatively SERS detection of MG and CV, Raman spectroscopic homogeneous assay was performed under optimized conditions. Enhanced SERS response of AgNPs/N-dots was observed upon the addition of MG or CV solution into SERS-based NPs colloids. As shown in Fig. 7A, the prominent characteristic bands for MG at 1173cm−1 is associated with the in-plane vibration of ring C-H, peaks at 1220 and 1365cm−1 are assigned to the C-H rocking and N-phenyl stretching, respectively. The sharp peak at 1401cm−1 is attributed to the ring stretching vibration, bands at 1590cm−1 and 1617cm−1 originate from the ring C-C stretching. One can clearly find from Fig. 7B that the SERS optical spectra of CV standard solutions were similar to MG, due to their similar chemical structures. The characteristic peaks of CV at 1174cm−1, 1366cm−1 and 1584/1619cm−1 are attributed to the in-plane vibration of ring C-H, N-phenyl stretching and ring C-C stretching (The detailed assignments of vibrational bands of MG and CV are shown in Table 2). As also shown in Fig. 7, as the concentration of MG and CV increase, the SERS signal intensity increases accordingly. Here, we picked out 1617cm−1 and 1619cm−1 for quantification. The SERS intensity at 1617cm−1 displayed an excellent linear correlation with MG concentration ranging from 10.0 to 1000.0nmol/L (R2 = 0.9958). A similar linearity was also found between the SERS intensity at 1619cm−1 and the concentrations of CV in the range of 5.0–100.0nmol/L (R2 = 0.9863). The detection limit (S/N = 3) were then calculated to be 2.7nmol/L for MG and 2.0nmol/L for CV, respectively. The above results demonstrated that AgNPs/N-dots showed very promising application in the detection of MG and CV.

To demonstrate the feasibility of practical use of our protocol in real water samples with low content of MG and CV, aquaculture water of food fish and ornamental fish were both used for SERS analysis. The aquaculture water used in this experiment is collected from Guangzhou, Guangdong province of China. After filtered through 0.22µm Millipore filters to remove any particulate suspension, the aquaculture water was treated with different concentrations of MG or CV. It can be seen from Table 3 that the recoveries were within the range of 80–113% for MG and 84–108% for CV with a RSD less than 12.5% under the optimal conditions. The result with good accuracy and reliability indicates that the established method using AgNPs/N-dots as SERS substrate is suitable for analysis of MG and CV in aquaculture water.

In summary, we have demonstrated a simple and fast approach towards the synthesis of AgNPs using N-dots as the reducing and stabilizing agent. This method took short time and mild temperature for the reduction of Ag+ to corresponding AgNPs, which is contributed to the excellent electron donating capability of N-dots with abundant nitrogen and oxygen -containing functional groups on their surface. Besides, the residual nitrogen and oxygen-containing groups and the peripheral on the N-dots made the obtained AgNPs/N-dots possess excellent dispersivity and long-term stability in water. Significantly, the electron transfer mechanism of N-dots will broaden their potential applications in the fields of energy-conversion systems or catalysis. Furthermore, the prepared AgNPs/N-dots nanocomposites can be served as an effective platform for SERS detection of MG and CV with LOD as low as 10−9 mol/L, indicating that the as-synthesized AgNPs/N-dots nanocomposites may hold potential applications in SERS.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21475153, 21575167 and 21675178), the Guangdong Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 2015A030311020 and 2016A030313358), and the Special Funds for Public Welfare Research and Capacity Building in Guangdong Province of China (No. 2015A030401036), and the Guangzhou Science and Technology Program of China (No. 201604020165), respectively.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2017.09.072.
